mechanism_narrative: Consistent positive growth in non-farm payrolls (PAYEMS) indicates a robust labor market and expanding economy, which typically translates into higher consumer spending and corporate earnings, driving positive S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: If PAYEMS shows consistent month-over-month growth for 6 consecutive months, but S&P 500 20-day returns are negative on average during this period, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: A federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) maintained below a low threshold signals an accommodative monetary policy, which reduces borrowing costs for corporations and consumers, thereby supporting equity valuations and leading to positive S&P 500 returns.
failure_condition: If FEDFUNDS remains below 0.25% for 6 consecutive months, but S&P 500 60-day returns are negative on average, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: Simultaneous strong growth in retail sales (RSAFS) and industrial production (INDPRO) indicates robust consumer demand and expanding manufacturing activity, signaling a healthy economic expansion that boosts corporate profits and drives S&P 500 prices higher.
candidate_trigger: RSAFS 3-month average growth > 0.5% MoM AND INDPRO 3-month average growth > 0.2% MoM
failure_condition: If both RSAFS and INDPRO show positive growth exceeding their respective thresholds for 3 consecutive months, but S&P 500 20-day returns are negative in more than 60% of such instances, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: A significant and sustained increase in the unemployment rate (UNRATE) signals a deteriorating labor market and broader economic contraction, leading to reduced consumer spending and corporate earnings, which drives S&P 500 forward returns lower.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE rises > 0.5 percentage points over 3 months
failure_condition: If UNRATE rises by more than 0.5 percentage points over 3 months during a period officially declared as a recession, but S&P 500 60-day returns are positive on average, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp increase in housing starts (HOUST) indicates strong confidence in the economic outlook and future demand, stimulating construction and related industries, which typically translates into positive S&P 500 returns over a short horizon.
failure_condition: If HOUST rises by more than 10% month-over-month, but S&P 500 5-day returns are negative in more than 50% of such occurrences over a 2-year rolling window, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: Accelerating consumer price inflation (CPIAUCSL) erodes corporate profit margins and may prompt the Federal Reserve to tighten monetary policy, increasing discount rates and negatively impacting equity valuations, leading to lower S&P 500 returns.
failure_condition: If CPIAUCSL shows an annualized 3-month change greater than 3%, but S&P 500 20-day returns are positive on average over any 12-month period, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: Sustained strong real GDP growth (GDPC1) reflects a robust and expanding economy, which directly translates into higher corporate revenues and profits, thereby driving positive S&P 500 forward returns as investor confidence increases.
failure_condition: If GDPC1 annualized growth exceeds 2.5% for two consecutive quarters, but S&P 500 60-day returns are negative in more than 40% of the subsequent periods, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: A combination of a very low unemployment rate (UNRATE) and a rising federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) signals a late-cycle economy where the Fed is actively tightening monetary policy to prevent overheating, which typically precedes a market slowdown or correction due to increased cost of capital.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE < 5.0% AND FEDFUNDS rises > 0.25 percentage points over 3 months
failure_condition: If UNRATE is below 5.0% and FEDFUNDS rises by more than 0.25 percentage points over 3 months, but S&P 500 60-day returns are positive on average over a 1-year period, the hypothesis is rejected.
